title: Real-time Astronomical Time-series Classification and Broadcast Pipeline
summary: A Python-based pipeline connecting synoptic-survey telescope data streams to real-time machine classification and event broadcast, from the Berkeley Transients Classification Pipeline project (with Dan Starr and John Brewer).
notes: Proceedings paper; talk co-presented with first author Dan Starr.

title: Building a Classification Engine for the Palomar Transient Factory
summary: Session talk on the machine-learning classification engine behind Palomar Transient Factory discoveries, in the event-classification session alongside LSST and Caltech colleagues.
notes: HTU-I, Tucson, June 4-7 2007; talk June 5 in the Event Classification session (IVOA TWiki agenda). Previously mislabeled as HTU-II 2009.

title: Machine Learning and Classification in the Synoptic Survey Era
summary: ML-driven discovery and classification of transients and variable stars in the era of synoptic surveys (PTF to LSST): real/bogus discrimination, probabilistic variable-star catalogs, and automating the discovery loop.
notes: 51-slide deck on SlideShare; exact venue/date uncertain (estimated from related 2011-2012 work).

title: Data-Mining and Machine Learning in the LSST Era
event: Royal Society Discussion Meeting: New Windows on Transients Across the Universe
summary: Invited discussion-meeting talk on machine learning as a surrogate for rapid human analysis at LSST data volumes: discovery algorithms, classification methodologies, and real-time transient identification.
notes: Deck on Speaker Deck confirms event and date.

title: Python as Super Glue for the Modern Scientific Workflow
summary: Invited talk on Python as the glue of the modern scientific workflow, from robotic-telescope automation and real-time transient-discovery pipelines to machine learning in astronomy.
notes: Listed on PyVideo; verify YouTube ID at build time.

title: Classification Challenges
summary: ML approaches to astronomical classification for LSST: real/bogus candidate discrimination, variable-star classification, and probabilistic cataloging, with examples from the Palomar Transient Factory including SN 2011fe.
notes: Deck on Speaker Deck (LSST AHM, Aug 13-17 2012); he was LSST Transients & Variable Stars co-chair.

title: The Modern Astrophysics Stack: Automated Action and Insight
event: Symposium on Visions of the Theory of Computing, Simons Institute
summary: Talk on the automated, ML-driven software stack of modern time-domain astrophysics at the Simons Institute's inaugural public symposium; reportedly the earliest video on the Simons Institute YouTube channel.
notes: Deck title slide says May 30, 2013 (symposium ran May 29-31; ledger previously said May 31). Video in the Simons collection; verify YouTube ID at build time.

title: Extracting Actionable Insight from Dirty Time-Series Data
event: UC Berkeley Data Science Lecture Series
summary: How to extract real-time, actionable insight from noisy, incomplete streaming sensor data (earthquakes, supernovae, traffic), and the interplay between domain scientists, statisticians, and computer scientists.
notes: Webcast archived on the BIDS video resources page.

title: Large-Scale Inference in Time Domain Astrophysics
event: Big Data Boot Camp, Simons Institute
summary: Boot-camp lecture on large-scale statistical inference for time-domain astronomy, during the Simons Institute's Theoretical Foundations of Big Data Analysis program.

title: Astrophysics of Streaming Time-Series Data: Discovery and Inference
summary: CITRIS Research Exchange seminar on ML pipelines for discovery and inference on streaming astronomical time-series data (variable stars, transients, synoptic surveys).
notes: Exact date within 2013 unconfirmed.

title: Overcoming the Barriers to Production-Ready Machine-Learning Workflows
summary: With Wise.io co-founder Henrik Brink: practical obstacles to deploying ML in production, including data preprocessing, scaling models to growing data, and deployment into production pipelines.

title: Computational Training and Data Literacy for Domain Scientists
event: Training Students to Extract Value from Big Data, National Academy of Sciences
summary: Invited lecture at the NAS workshop on big-data education, drawing on the Berkeley 'Python for Data Science' bootcamps and course, and what domain scientists need in computational training; proceedings published by National Academies Press.
notes: Slides title matched to this event (probable).

title: Data Science at Berkeley
summary: Keynote at Facebook HQ on the emergence of data science at UC Berkeley: training, the newly founded Berkeley Institute for Data Science, and the interplay of theory-driven and data-driven inference in scientific practice.

title: Machine Learning in Astronomy
summary: Colloquium on applying statistical machine learning to large-scale astronomy datasets in batch and streaming modes, as co-chair of the LSST Transients and Variable Stars collaboration.

title: UC Berkeley Data Science Startups
event: DataEDGE Conference, UC Berkeley School of Information
summary: Panelist (as co-founder of Wise.io) discussing the wave of data-science startups emerging from UC Berkeley research.

title: Supervised Machine Learning
summary: Morning lecture on supervised ML for astronomers — classification and regression, random forests, and a scikit-learn demo on SDSS star/galaxy/quasar data — at the first Astro Hack Week.

title: Data Science Education: Needs & Opportunities in Astronomy
summary: On the growing need for data-science training in astronomy curricula and the opportunities for cross-disciplinary education, from Berkeley bootcamps to institute-scale efforts.
notes: 25-slide deck on SlideShare; exact venue/date uncertain.

title: Machine Learning in Production
summary: As Wise.io co-founder/CTO: what it takes to deploy machine learning in production enterprise settings, and how industrial ML practice differs from academic ML.
notes: Month per FirstMark slide upload (May 2015); one listing says Nov 2015.

title: A Systems View of Machine Learning
summary: Keynote at the Microsoft campus arguing that real-world ML must be understood as an end-to-end system — data ingestion, featurization, modeling, deployment, and feedback — not just algorithms.

title: Machine Learning for Businesses
summary: On deploying ML in enterprise software: why ML is not a drop-in module, data-scientist stratification, abstraction layers for ML applications, and technical debt in ML systems.

title: Machine Learning (lecture)
summary: Tutorial lecture on machine-learning methods for astronomers (classification, regression, feature engineering) at Astro Hack Week 2016, hosted at UC Berkeley and recorded by BIDS.
notes: Exact day within Aug 29-Sep 2 approximate.

title: Machine Learning for the Stars & Productizing AI
summary: An 84-minute interview on pioneering ML for robotic-telescope imagery at Berkeley, the founding and evolution of Wise.io, its data-science pipeline, and open challenges in machine learning.

title: Industrial Machine Learning
event: UC Berkeley Haas School of Business
summary: Guest lecture at Berkeley Haas on industrial-scale machine learning — deploying ML against industrial data at GE Digital after the Wise.io acquisition, and the gaps between academic, startup, and enterprise ML.
notes: Year approximate.

title: Marrying Physics-Based and Data-Driven ML Models
summary: Follow-up interview after the Wise.io acquisition: Industrial AI at GE Digital, autoencoders for building training datasets, and combining physics-based models with data-driven ML.

title: Industrial Machine Learning
event: KDD 2017, Applied Data Science Invited Talks
summary: Applied Data Science invited talk (as VP of Data & Analytics, GE Digital) on deploying machine learning against industrial data at scale.
notes: Day within Aug 13-17 approximate.

title: Autoencoding RNNs for Inference on Unevenly Sampled Time-Series
event: Workshop on Applying Advanced AI Workflows in Astronomy and Microscopy
summary: Presenting the recurrent autoencoder network for classifying unevenly sampled variable-star light curves (Naul, Bloom, Perez & van der Walt; Nature Astronomy 2018).
notes: Exact date/venue from the deck title slide (11 Sep 2018, Santa Clara). Deck matches Naul, Bloom, Perez & van der Walt, Nature Astronomy 2, 151 (2018). Same talk also at #ML4Sci LBNL Sep 5 and NCSA Oct 17, 2018 (separate entries).

title: Industrial Machine Learning
summary: Keynote arguing that the real machine-learning revolution — in improved and saved lives — will come when ML automation is coupled with industrial data.

title: Machine Learning for Time-Domain Astrophysics
event: Real-Time Decision Making program, Simons Institute
summary: How statistical ML applies to astronomy in batch and streaming contexts: feature-engineered supernova identification and variable-star characterization, and autoencoder/RNN architectures that learn without hand-built features.
notes: He also co-organized this semester-long program.

title: Industrial-Grade Machine Learning
summary: From astrophysics to industrial ML at GE Digital: bridging physics-driven and data-driven models, bias, trustworthy deployment, and ML on private data across competing customers.
notes: Month approximate. Episode delisted everywhere as of 2026-07-18 (Apple 404 in GB and US, absent from iTunes episode index, rolled off the Wistia feed, no Wayback capture) — audio unrecoverable; event_url now points at the show page (old Apple GB link dead).

title: Astrophysical Machine Learning
event: Berkeley Institute for Data Science (BIDS) Data Science Lecture
summary: How time-domain astronomers use ML on streaming, noisy, distorted images of the sky — the search for Planet 9, discovery of new variable sources, data-driven emulators — and building physics-informed learning architectures.

title: Optimizing DESI for the Time-Domain Opportunities
summary: Plenary on how the DESI spectroscopic survey could benefit time-domain astronomy — variable stars, transients, circumnuclear events, unusual supernovae, and multimessenger follow-up — with proposed fiber-allocation and data-access strategies.

title: Machine Learning at Scale: Astrophysics
event: DOE AI for Science Town Hall, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
summary: Plenary at the DOE 'AI for Science' town hall on scaling ML for astrophysics — real-time discovery, classification, and inference over massive survey data streams — feeding into the 2020 DOE AI for Science report.

title: Physics-Informed (and -Informative) Generative Modelling in Astronomy
event: Machine Learning for Physics and the Physics of Learning, IPAM (UCLA)
summary: Practical generative neural models in astronomy: semi-supervised VAEs mapping physical parameters to latent space for telescope scheduling, cosmic-ray artifact detection and inpainting (deepCR), and autoencoder-based semantic indexing for compressed sensing.
notes: IPAM 'From Passive to Active' workshop Sep 23-27, 2019; talk Sep 23 (CV's Nov 2019 is incorrect).

title: Towards Physics-Informed ML Inference in Astrophysics
summary: At the Computer History Museum: building ML inference for astrophysics that respects and exploits physical models, with applications to time-domain surveys.
notes: Day within Feb 26-27 approximate.

title: Physics-Informed Machine Learning in Astronomy
summary: ML across astronomy workflows: the Planet 9 search, new variable-source classes, semi-supervised VAEs generating realistic time series to optimize observing schedules, and deep-learning cosmic-ray detection and correction.
notes: Originally scheduled Apr 17, 2020 in person (cancelled, COVID); delivered virtually Oct 2020 per CV. IACS listing title confirmed.

title: Machine Learning in Astronomical Surveys (ML Club intro)
summary: Overview of ML in astronomical surveys framed around Rubin/LSST: real-bogus classification, inpainting for artifact removal, anomaly detection, calibrated Bayesian classification, similarity search at scale, and MLOps for reproducible science.

title: The Link Between Astronomy and ML
summary: As Berkeley astronomy chair, with host Lukas Biewald: why astronomers were early ML adopters, real/bogus detection, uncertainty quantification, likelihood-free inference, and why ML hasn't displaced domain expertise.

title: AI Assisted Discovery: from UX to Eureka!
event: Cosmic Connections: ML X Astrophysics Symposium, Simons Foundation
summary: How AI/ML has uniquely contributed to novel science in astronomy across three assistive modes: circumventing human-centric bottlenecks, accelerating physics-based computation, and hypothesis generation — including LLMs for UX and a simulation-based-inference breakthrough in microlensing degeneracy theory.
notes: At the Flatiron Institute's Ingrid Daubechies Auditorium; video in Day 2 Session 1 recording.

title: The Real AI Revolution in Astronomy Hasn't Happened Yet
event: A3D3 Institute Seminar / UW Physics Colloquium
summary: Astronomy has embraced AI for data analysis, but the real revolution will integrate AI across the full scientific workflow — telescope design and real-time control upstream, hypothesis generation downstream — via simulation-based inference, self-supervised learning, foundation models, neural compression, and RL.
notes: Given as both the A3D3 seminar and UW Physics colloquium the same day.

title: AI Accelerating Inquiry and Insight in Astrophysics
summary: How the astrophysical data deluge has swamped traditional workflows and driven AI/ML tooling: real-time telescope control, survey optimization, simulation-based inference, multimodal foundation models (AstroM3), active optics, and neural compression (AstroCompress).

title: Eurekaizing Anomalies
event: Center for Decoding the Universe Annual Conference, Stanford
summary: Faculty talk in the opening session on time-domain astronomical data and anomaly detection — finding rare and novel transients and variables in large survey data streams with machine learning.
notes: CV title; video was researched under working title 'Time-Domain Data and Anomaly Detection'. Day within June 5-6 approximate.

title: Gamma Ray Bursters
summary: Ira Flatow interviews Bloom about the origin of short gamma-ray bursts, following the first localization of a short GRB (GRB 050509b): the evidence that short bursts come from merging neutron stars rather than the deaths of massive stars.
notes: NPR transcript available. Direct NPR MP3 and npr.org/transcripts page linked (recovered 2026-07-18).

title: In a Flash of Gamma-Rays, a Star Is Gone
summary: The day after publication of the Science paper on Swift J1644+57, Ira Flatow interviews Bloom (with Lawrence Krauss) on the gamma-ray outburst interpreted as a supermassive black hole shredding and swallowing a star 3.8 billion light-years away.
notes: 12-minute segment; NPR transcript available. Direct NPR MP3 and npr.org/transcripts page linked (recovered 2026-07-18).

title: Black Hole Swallows a Star (Swift J1644+57)
summary: BBC Newshour interview on the discovery of a possible relativistic jetted outburst from a massive black hole fed by a tidally disrupted star.
notes: Linked as MP3 from his old Berkeley homepage; original audio link now offline.

title: Telescope Targets Black Holes' Binges and Burps
summary: Featured expert in Lauren Sommer's piece on NASA's newly launched NuSTAR X-ray telescope, explaining black-hole 'binges and burps' and debunking the vacuum-cleaner misconception.
notes: Direct NPR MP3 and npr.org/transcripts page linked (recovered 2026-07-18).

title: The Corrected Log N–Log Fluence Distribution of Cosmological Gamma-Ray Bursts
summary: With Fenimore and in 't Zand: correcting the observed log N-log fluence distribution of BATSE bursts for detection biases, an early constraint on the cosmological burst population.
notes: Proceedings: AIP Conf. Proc. 384, 321 (1996), DOI 10.1063/1.51552; arXiv linked.

title: Extragalactic Hosts
event: Latest Developments in Gamma-Ray Bursts Workshop
summary: On the extragalactic host galaxies of gamma-ray bursts, in the first post-BeppoSAX year as afterglows and hosts were first being identified.
notes: Workshop May 26-27, 1997, Marciana Marina, Elba (~80 attendees); no proceedings published. Program page live at HEASARC.

title: The Host to GRB 970508: A High-Redshift Dwarf Galaxy?
summary: Evidence that the host of GRB 970508 - the first burst with a measured redshift - is a distant dwarf galaxy, tying bursts to faint star-forming hosts.
notes: Symposium Sept 15-20, 1997 (CV said Oct). Result published as Natarajan, Bloom, Sigurdsson et al. 1997, New Astronomy 2, 471 (linked; no arXiv version).

title: The Spatial Distribution of Coalescing Neutron Star Binaries: Implications for Gamma-Ray Bursts
summary: Population-synthesis predictions for where merging neutron-star binaries occur relative to their host galaxies, and what burst offsets imply for the merger progenitor model.
notes: CV lists 10 Jan 1997, but AAS 191 was Jan 6-10, 1998 in DC (AAS 189 was Jan 1997 Toronto). Linked arXiv is the refereed version (Bloom, Sigurdsson & Pols 1999, MNRAS 305, 763).

title: The Discovery and Broad-Band Follow-Up of the Transient Afterglow of GRB 980703
event: Gamma-Ray Bursts in the Afterglow Era, 1st Workshop
summary: The discovery and multi-wavelength follow-up of GRB 980703's afterglow and its dusty star-forming host galaxy.
notes: Talk title matches Bloom et al. 1998, ApJ 508, L21 (linked); no separate proceedings version.

title: GRBs & SNe: Investigation of GRB 980519 and GRB 980329
summary: Searching for supernova signatures underlying the afterglows of GRB 980519 and GRB 980329, early evidence bearing on the burst-supernova connection.
notes: Symposium Oct 18-22, 1999; session A-11. Live NASA program page linked; Wayback abstract also exists.

title: Emerging Trends in Gamma-Ray Bursts
summary: Survey of the rapidly moving observational GRB field for the IPAC astronomy community.
notes: No surviving listing found (2026-07 search).

title: GRB Host Galaxies: Hints for the Models
summary: What the properties of GRB host galaxies imply for progenitor models, at the relativistic-astrophysics meeting.
notes: Own parallel-session talk was never separately indexed; linked arXiv is the co-authored MG9 GRB review (Djorgovski, Frail, Kulkarni, Sari, Bloom et al., pp. 315-346).

title: The Observed Offset Distribution of GRBs About Their Hosts
event: Gamma-Ray Bursts in the Afterglow Era, 2nd Workshop
summary: The measured offsets of gamma-ray bursts from their host galaxies as a direct test of progenitor models - massive stars versus compact-object mergers.
notes: Proceedings: Bloom & Kulkarni 2001, pp. 209-211 (Springer DOI linked); full study is Bloom, Kulkarni & Djorgovski 2002, AJ 123, 1111 (arXiv:astro-ph/0010176).

title: Toward the Progenitors of Gamma-Ray Bursts
summary: Progenitor evidence for long and short gamma-ray bursts from afterglow positions, host demographics, and supernova signatures.
notes: CfA OIR lunch seminar (not a departmental colloquium); live page gone, Wayback snapshot linked.

title: Toward the Progenitors of Gamma-Ray Bursts
summary: Progenitor evidence for gamma-ray bursts - offsets, hosts, and the emerging supernova connection - presented at the IAS.
notes: Invited. Not on the archived IAS formal seminar list (a Friday); likely an informal astrophysics talk; no recoverable listing.

title: The Progenitors of Gamma-Ray Bursts
summary: Invited review of gamma-ray burst progenitor evidence: afterglow offsets, host galaxies, and supernova bumps.
notes: Slides survive only as a Wayback capture of the 43-slide web export from his old Caltech pages (linked); his archived talks page lists the title as 'Towards the Progenitors...'.

title: Optical/IR Afterglow Observations
summary: Review of optical and infrared afterglow observations at the Harvard Sackler conference on gamma-ray bursts.
notes: Conference May 20-23, 2002. Old slides at astro.caltech.edu/~jsb/sackler-review were never archived (Wayback 404s); no recoverable page.

title: The Case for Late Optical Bumps as Associated Supernovae
event: Gamma-Ray Bursts in the Afterglow Era, 3rd Workshop
summary: Solicited case that late-time optical bumps in GRB afterglows are underlying supernovae - the photometric argument for the massive-star origin of long bursts.
notes: Proceedings version on arXiv (Bloom 2003) linked.

title: Energetics and the GRB Hubble Diagram
summary: Standardizing gamma-ray burst energies and the promises and limitations of a GRB Hubble diagram for cosmology.
notes: Proceedings: AIP Conf. Proc. 727, 497 (2004); arXiv version linked ('GRB Energetics and the GRB Hubble Diagram: Promises and Limitations').

title: Optical/Infrared Observations of Gamma-Ray Bursts and X-Ray Flash Transients
event: University of Texas at Austin Colloquium
summary: Colloquium on optical/IR follow-up of gamma-ray bursts and the newly identified X-ray flashes.
notes: No surviving listing found.

title: Cosmological Gamma-Ray Bursts: Perspectives and Prospects
summary: Colloquium on the state of cosmological gamma-ray burst studies, delivered shortly before joining the Berkeley faculty.
notes: No surviving listing found.

title: Optical/IR Observations of GRB & XRF Transients
summary: Colloquium on optical and infrared observations of gamma-ray burst and X-ray flash transients.
notes: No surviving listing found.

title: GRB-Supernova Connection: Evidence and Implications
summary: The observational case connecting long gamma-ray bursts to supernovae and its implications for progenitors.
notes: 'Texas @ Stanford', Dec 13-17, 2004; SLAC eConf proceedings index linked (individual contribution not identified). slac.stanford.edu rejects non-browser fetches; Wayback snapshot linked instead.

title: New Perspectives on Gamma-Ray Bursts
summary: Colloquium on gamma-ray bursts in the first months of the Swift era.
notes: No surviving listing found.

title: Implementing a Real-Time VOEvent Messaging Network
summary: Design and implementation of a real-time astronomical event messaging network, at the workshop that launched the VOEvent standard.
notes: Caltech CACR, April 13-14, 2005. Slides PDF and QuickTime talk movie both still live on the IVOA wiki.

title: PAIRITEL Science Overview and Commissioning Update
summary: Science goals and commissioning status of PAIRITEL, the robotic 1.3-m infrared telescope for transient follow-up.
notes: No workshop page survives (pairitel.org Wayback captures lack it).

title: Shedding Light on Short Gamma-Ray Burst Progenitors
event: INPA Seminar, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
summary: The first afterglow localizations of short gamma-ray bursts and what they reveal about compact-merger progenitors, weeks after the breakthrough events.
notes: CV lists 'IPNA'; presumably the LBNL INPA (Institute for Nuclear and Particle Astrophysics) seminar. No listing found.

title: Gamma-Ray Bursts and Their Hosts
event: 16th Maryland Astrophysics Conference: Gamma-Ray Bursts in the Swift Era
summary: Invited review of gamma-ray burst host galaxies and large-scale environments as progenitor diagnostics, in the first year of Swift.
notes: Conference Nov 29-Dec 2, 2005. Proceedings: Bloom & Prochaska 2006, AIP Conf. Proc. 836, 473 (arXiv linked).

title: The Hosts of (Short) Gamma-Ray Bursts
event: SWIFT and GRBs: Unveiling the Relativistic Universe
summary: Solicited talk on the galaxy hosts and environments of short gamma-ray bursts and the case for an old-population progenitor.
notes: Conference June 5-9, 2006; no separate proceedings contribution - linked arXiv is the closely related Bloom et al. 2006, ApJ 642, 989.

title: The Host Galaxies of Short Gamma-Ray Bursts
event: 9th AAS High Energy Astrophysics Division Meeting
summary: Invited review of short-burst host galaxies at the HEAD meeting, cementing the demographic case for compact-object mergers.
notes: No program page or ADS abstract recoverable for this HEAD meeting talk.

title: Gamma-Ray Bursts: The Long and the Short of Them
summary: Colloquium on the two burst populations - collapsars and mergers - and the evidence separating them.
notes: No archived spring-2007 Princeton colloquium schedule exists.

title: GRBs in a Cosmological Context
summary: Colloquium on using gamma-ray bursts as probes of the distant universe.
notes: Listed in the Leiden Observatory 2008 annual report (linked, colloquia appendix), printed there as 'GRBs in a Cosmology Context'.

title: SASIR: The Synoptic All-Sky Infrared Imaging Survey Concept
event: Instituto de Astrofisica de Andalucia Seminar
summary: The concept for a synoptic all-sky infrared imaging survey (SASIR), a proposed 6.5-m IR survey telescope.
notes: Invited. No IAA seminar page survives; linked arXiv is the SASIR concept paper (Bloom et al. 2009).

title: GRBs as Cosmological Probes
summary: Gamma-ray bursts as probes of reionization and the high-redshift universe, at the Harvard Sackler 21-cm cosmology conference.
notes: Month per CV. Linked page is the Sackler series page (2008 program subpage not recoverable; blocks bots but live in browser).

title: The Future of Gamma-Ray Bursts
summary: Where gamma-ray burst science is headed, at the symposium honoring Vahe Petrosian.
notes: Event confirmed via participant CVs; no program page survives.

title: The Synoptic Infrared Imaging Survey
summary: The SASIR synoptic infrared survey concept, presented at the second Hot-wiring the Transient Universe workshop.
notes: HTU-II at UCO/Lick, UC Santa Cruz, April 26-30, 2009; talk opened Session 1 on April 27. Original site dead - Wayback program page linked; his slides PDF was never archived.

title: Real-Time Classification in the Petascale Epoch: Ramping Up with PTF
summary: Machine-learned real-time classification of Palomar Transient Factory events as the ramp toward petascale synoptic surveys.
notes: Aspen Center for Physics summer workshop 'Wide-Fast-Deep Surveys: New Astrophysics Frontier', June 14-July 5, 2009; no per-talk agenda survives (workshop listed inline on linked page).

title: Finding Utility in the Diverse Origins of Gamma-Ray Bursts
event: AAS 215th Meeting - Newton Lacy Pierce Prize Lecture
summary: The Pierce Prize lecture: what the diverse progenitors of gamma-ray bursts - collapsars, mergers, and oddballs - teach us, and how bursts became tools for cosmology.
notes: 2009 Pierce Prize, delivered at AAS 215 (Jan 3-7, 2010), Session 103. AAS meeting page blocks bots but is live.

title: Real-Time Classification of Astronomical Events
event: SIAM Conference on Computational Science & Engineering
summary: Machine-learning pipelines for classifying astronomical events in real time, for the computational science community.
notes: CSE11, Feb 28-Mar 4, 2011; minisymposium invited talk (not a plenary). Site blocks bots but is live.

title: Relativistic Tidal Disruption Events
summary: Colloquium on Swift J1644+57 and the discovery of relativistic tidal disruption events, months after the Science paper.
notes: No archived fall-2011 MIT colloquium schedule found.

title: Relativistic Black Hole Tidal Disruption Events
summary: Colloquium on relativistic tidal disruption events at STScI.
notes: CV says Nov 11; JHU Gazette listing (linked; blocks bots but live) gives Wed Nov 9, 2011, Bahcall Auditorium. No recording located in the STScI webcast archive.

title: Relativistic Tidal Disruption Events
summary: Yale Physics Club colloquium on relativistic tidal disruption events.
notes: CV says Dec 2012; Yale archive (linked) confirms Dec 3, 2012, host Nikhil Padmanabhan.

title: Automating Science in the Time-Domain Survey Era: Machine-Learning Challenges
summary: Machine-learning challenges in automating discovery and classification for time-domain surveys, at the SAMSI massive-datasets program.
notes: Workshop Sept 19-21, 2012; talk Sept 21. samsi.info is dead (domain squatted) - Wayback page linked; no slides posted for this talk.

title: Automated Astrophysics in the Big Data Era
event: SIAM Conference on Computational Science & Engineering
summary: Invited plenary (IP6) on automating astrophysical discovery, classification, and follow-up at survey scale.
notes: CSE13, Feb 25-Mar 1, 2013; day approximate. Linked SIAM talk page requires a SIAM sign-in for the recording; the old direct slides PDF is dead with no Wayback copy.

title: Big Machine Learning
summary: Machine learning at astronomical scale, in the Big Data session of KIPAC's tenth-anniversary symposium.
notes: KIPAC@10, Sept 3-6, 2013; talk Sept 5 in the 'How Will We Know What We Know?' Big Data session. KIPAC video linked; original event page via Wayback. Original event page has no Wayback capture; event_url dropped.

title: Astrophysical Applications of Machine Learning at Scale and Under Duress
event: IEEE International Parallel & Distributed Processing Symposium
summary: IPDPS keynote on machine learning for astronomical discovery under real-time and scale constraints.
notes: Keynote Thursday May 22, 2014, 8:30am. CV lists Flagstaff and a shorter title; official program (linked) says Phoenix and the full title. No video or slides posted.

title: Large-Scale Inference in Time Domain Astrophysics
event: MMDS Workshop on Algorithms for Modern Massive Data Sets
summary: Statistical inference on massive time-domain astronomy datasets, for the algorithms-for-massive-data community.
notes: MMDS 2014, June 17-20, UC Berkeley; talk June 19. Video on the MMDS Foundation channel; mmds-data.org is currently down - Wayback linked. Slides were never successfully posted.

title: Computational and Data Literacy for Domain Scientists
summary: How universities can train domain scientists in computation and data science, in the AAAS symposium on interdisciplinary data-intensive career paths.
notes: CV says Jan 2015; the 2015 AAAS meeting ran Feb 12-16, San Jose - talk Feb 15 in 'Advancing University Career Paths in Interdisciplinary Data-Intensive Science' (with Lazowska, Aragon, Freire, Perez, Howe).

title: Emerging Needs and Opportunities in Data-Intensive Domains: Astronomy
event: National Academies Roundtable on Data Science Postsecondary Education
summary: Astronomy's data-science needs and opportunities, for the National Academies roundtable on data science education.
notes: Meeting #2 of the roundtable, Beckman Center, March 20, 2017; closed session - only the written proceedings (linked) exist.

title: Systems of Intelligence
summary: Keynote on building systems of intelligence - coupling machine learning with industrial data and workflows.
notes: Interop ITX 2017, May 15-19, MGM Grand. The keynote itself was not published; the only footage featuring him is the 'Future of Data' lightning-talks video (youtube.com/watch?v=h1GWKcBD2-I). Official presentations portal is dead with no Wayback copy.

title: 50 Years of Gamma-Ray Bursts
event: Gehrels Memorial Meeting, NASA Goddard Space Flight Center
summary: A half-century of gamma-ray burst science, honoring Neil Gehrels at the Goddard memorial meeting.
notes: CV lists 'Gehrels Memorial Lecture, NAS' - the meeting was at GSFC May 21-22, 2018 (meals at NAS); talk May 21 AM. No video posted.

title: An Autoencoding Recurrent Neural Network for Inference on Unevenly Sampled Time-Series Data
event: Deep Learning for Multimessenger Astrophysics: Real-Time Discovery at Scale, NCSA
summary: Autoencoding RNNs for classification and inference on irregularly sampled astronomical time series.
notes: Workshop name and exact date (17 Oct 2018) from the deck title slide; NCSA, University of Illinois.

title: Foundation Models in Production
event: Foundation Models for Astrophysics Workshop, Flatiron Institute
summary: Deploying astronomical foundation models in production settings, at the Flatiron workshop on foundation models for astrophysics.
notes: Invite-only workshop; no public page or recording found (2026-07 search).

title: AI Accelerating Astrophysics
summary: How AI/ML tooling is reshaping astrophysical workflows swamped by the data deluge, from discovery to inference at scale.
notes: Invited seminar; host Dimitrios Psaltis. Listed on the GT AI4Science fall 2025 archive; no recording posted. Identified via correspondence (2026-07 Gmail/Calendar sweep).

title: AI in Astronomy, a Primer
summary: A primer on how astronomy came to lean on AI/ML for discovery and inference at scale, for a multi-speaker Berkeley roundtable.
notes: Internal multi-speaker event (with P. Ma, P. Nugent, J. Somalwar, U. Seljak); no public page. Identified via correspondence (2026-07 sweep).

title: Circumventing Bottlenecks and Taking Better Data in the Time Domain
summary: ML across the time-domain pipeline: relieving discovery and follow-up bottlenecks and actively taking better data with Rubin/LSST-era surveys.
notes: Invited; KIPAC colloquium committee (S.E. Clark). Video is a Zoom recording share, passcode $50FQK3L (published on the page per JSB). Identified via correspondence (2026-07 sweep).

title: Time-Domain Astrophysics, Accelerated with AI
event: Inaugural Keystone Astronomy & AI (KAAI) / Simons Colloquium, Carnegie Mellon
summary: Inaugural KAAI colloquium: simulation-based inference, self-supervised multi-modal foundation models, Rubin/LSST active optics, and reinforcement learning for gravitational-wave target-of-opportunity scheduling.
notes: Inaugural talk of the Simons-funded KAAI series at the McWilliams Center (hosts Di Matteo & Palmese); Wean Hall 8330, 3:30pm. No video posted. Identified via correspondence (2026-07 sweep).

title: Foundation Models and Agents for Physics
event: PAI26: 2026 Conference on Physics and AI, Stanford
summary: Panel on foundation models and AI agents for physics, with Mariel Pettee and Ioana Ciuca.
notes: Listed as Josh Bloom (Valency/UC Berkeley); panel June 11, 1:45pm. Conference June 10-12. Video: Stanford HAI YouTube ("2026 Conference on Physics and AI: Panel 2", playlist PLYLBSCrrqNXwkVgUzHusgyRSuJWJz6HEs). Transcript from auto-captions.

title: CuratingAI Art Exhibition: Closing Panel
summary: Founder and curator of the CuratingAI exhibition, leading the closing panel on whether artists working with generative AI tools are creators, curators, or collaborators (panelists including Fernando Perez).
notes: Exhibition Apr 20-27, 2024; closing panel Apr 27, 4:45-5:45pm (Eventbrite: eventbrite.com/e/887351852277). Own write-up: joshbloom.org/post/curatingai/. Confirmed by JSB (led the panel).

title: A Redress of Short Bursts
summary: The revised observational picture of short gamma-ray bursts five years into the Swift mission, with the Berkeley GRB group.
notes: Swift@5 conference, Penn State, Nov 18-20 2009; archived program lists the talk in the GRBs II: Observations session. Deck in local archive.

title: EXIST: Probing the Epoch of Reionization with Gamma-Ray Bursts
summary: The proposed EXIST hard X-ray survey mission as a probe of the reionization epoch through high-redshift gamma-ray bursts, as chair of the GRB-EXIST working group.
notes: Assembly Jul 18-25, 2010; day approximate. With Grindlay (PI) and the EXIST team; ADS abstract linked (also in the site publications). Deck in local archive.

title: The Transient Universe
summary: The dynamic sky - explosions, flares, and ephemeral events - for the SACNAS community of Chicano/Hispanic and Native American scientists in training.
notes: 2010 SACNAS National Conference, Anaheim, Sept 30-Oct 3. Deck in local archive.

title: High-Redshift GRBs with Lobster
summary: Using the proposed Lobster wide-field X-ray transient monitor to find the highest-redshift gamma-ray bursts.
notes: Lobster-eye X-ray mission concept meeting; no public meeting page survives. Deck in local archive.

title: SASIR: A Wide-Field Synoptic Survey for this Decade
event: AAS Town Hall Meeting, UC Berkeley
summary: The Synoptic All-Sky Infrared Survey concept, presented at a post-decadal-survey community town hall (alongside an LSST status report from Tony Tyson).
notes: Event as titled on the deck; a local community town hall at Berkeley after the Astro2010 decadal release - no public listing found. SASIR concept paper: arXiv:0905.1965. Deck in local archive.

title: Constraints on the Progenitor System of the Type Ia Supernova SN 2011fe/PTF11kly
summary: Ruling out red-giant and most main-sequence companions for the nearest modern Type Ia supernova, weeks after its discovery (Li, Bloom et al., Nature 2011).
notes: Deck title slide says 'IAU Oxford - 20 Sept 2011'; no matching formal IAU meeting exists, so most plausibly an Oxford departmental seminar (co-author Podsiadlowski is at Oxford). Result: Nature 480, 348 (arXiv:1109.1593). Deck in local archive.

title: Constraints on the Progenitor System of the Type Ia Supernova SN 2011fe/PTF11kly
summary: The SN 2011fe progenitor constraints presented to the AAS winter meeting.
notes: Deck footer reads 'AAS Austin - Jan 2011', a typo: AAS 219 was Jan 8-12, 2012 (SN 2011fe was discovered Aug 2011); day approximate. AAS page blocks bots but is live. Deck in local archive.

title: GRB Phenomenology & Neutrinos
summary: Gamma-ray burst phenomenology and the prospects for coincident neutrino detection, for the IceCube collaboration.
notes: IceCube Spring 2012 collaboration meeting, Berkeley (confirmed via IceCube M&O FY12 report); agenda is on a login-walled legacy Indico, so no public page. Deck in local archive.

title: Co-ordinating Facilities
event: Interpreting Signals from Astrophysical Transient Experiments, Kavli Royal Society Centre
summary: Discussion lead on coordinating follow-up facilities across transient experiments, at the Kavli satellite meeting following the Royal Society transients discussion meeting.
notes: Satellite of the Apr 23-24 London meeting (see royal-society-2012); Chicheley Hall, Apr 25-26, 2012. Archived page lists him as a speaker; live royalsociety.org page is gone. Proceedings: Phil. Trans. R. Soc. A 371, 1992. Deck in local archive.

title: Extracting Novel Insight from Probabilistic Machine-Learned Classification Catalogs
event: SIAM Conference on Computational Science & Engineering
summary: Doing science with probabilistic machine-learned classification catalogs, in the Big Data minisymposium (MS158) - his second CSE13 talk besides the plenary.
notes: MS158, Wed Feb 27, 2013, 6:00pm (program PDF linked; the plenary is siam-cse-2013). Deck in local archive.

title: Data-Driven Astronomical Inference with Machine Learning
summary: Machine-learned inference for astronomical surveys, at the UC-HiPACC exascale computational astrophysics workshop at LBNL.
notes: Workshop Mar 21-22, 2014, LBNL Bldg 66; agenda lists the talk (23 min). Workshop videos in a YouTube playlist linked from the page (per-talk links broken). Deck in local archive.

title: Data Science Infrastructure & Time-Domain Inference
event: Moore Foundation Data-Driven Discovery Investigator Symposium
summary: Data-science infrastructure and time-domain inference work, at the Moore Foundation DDD investigators meeting.
notes: Invitation-only investigator symposium; no public agenda exists. Deck in local archive.

title: Machine Learning in Production: Trials, Tribulations & Triumphs
summary: Lessons from putting machine learning into production at wise.io, for the SF Machine Learning meetup hosted at Instacart.
notes: Meetup event page purged and never archived; linked deck is the same-material Data Driven NYC upload (cf. data-driven-nyc-2015). Deck in local archive.

title: Astrophysical Insights in the Presence of Uncertainty & Under Duress
summary: Astrophysical inference under noise, uncertainty, and real-time pressure, at the Simons Foundation.
notes: Deck title slide says 'May 15' without a year; ~2015 inferred from branding and not independently verifiable - no event page or video found. Deck in local archive.

title: Time-Domain Inference, Data Science Tooling, & Industrial Machine Learning
event: Moore Foundation Data-Driven Discovery Investigator Symposium
summary: Spanning time-domain astrophysics, open-source tooling, and industrial ML at GE, for the Moore DDD investigators meeting.
notes: Invitation-only symposium; location/date beyond 2018 not publicly documented. Deck in local archive.

title: Time-Domain Inference and Physics in ML
event: Moore Foundation Data-Driven Discovery Investigator Symposium
summary: Physics-informed machine learning for time-domain inference, at the Moore DDD investigators meeting.
notes: Invitation-only symposium; location/date beyond 2019 not publicly documented. Deck in local archive.

title: Autoencoding RNN for Inference on Unevenly Sampled Time-Series Data
event: #ML4Sci: Machine Learning for Science Workshop, LBNL
summary: The autoencoding-RNN approach to irregular astronomical time series, at the LBNL machine-learning-for-science workshop.
notes: First of three Sep-Oct 2018 deliveries of this material (see autoencoding-rnn-2018 and ncsa-2018); date/venue from the deck title slide. Deck in local archive.

title: Physics-Informed Astrophysical Machine Learning
event: Bay Area Scientific Computing Day, LBNL
summary: Keynote on physics-informed ML across astrophysics: the Planet 9 search, variable-source detection, generative telescope scheduling, and cosmic-ray removal.
notes: Listed as keynote on the BASCD 2019 page (linked). Deck in local archive.

title: Physics-Informed Machine Learning for Inference & Discovery
event: MLSE 2020: Machine Learning in Science & Engineering
summary: Physics-informed ML for inference and discovery, in the astronomy track of the Columbia-organized MLSE conference.
notes: Virtual, Dec 14-15, 2020 (Columbia Data Science Institute); conference splash site blocks bots - Columbia listing linked. Deck in local archive.

title: The Real AI Revolution in Astronomy Hasn't Happened Yet
event: CITRIS People and Robots / DREAM Seminar, UC Berkeley
summary: Why astronomy's AI transformation is still ahead - the earliest delivery of the argument later given as the A3D3/UW colloquium.
notes: CITRIS+DREAM joint seminar (Mondays, Sutardja Dai Hall); series page blocks bots but is live. Earlier instance of the a3d3-2024 talk. Deck in local archive.

title: A Surprising Discovery Using SBI in Exoplanets
summary: A simulation-based-inference result in exoplanet microlensing (with K. Zhang and collaborators), at the AI-in-astronomy splinter session of AAS 241.
notes: AAS 241, Seattle, Jan 8-12, 2023; splinter session not separately listed online (AAS pages block bots). Deck in local archive.

title: AI in Astrophysics
event: C2OA2SE Workshop, Michigan Tech Research Institute
summary: AI across astrophysics, at the cross-disciplinary workshop on AI applications to science and engineering.
notes: Workshop on Cross-Disciplinary Challenges and Opportunities in AI Applications to Science and Engineering, Nov 6-8, 2024 (MTRI, Ann Arbor); talk Nov 7, 1:30pm per the page. Deck in local archive.

title: Accelerating Discovery and Inference with Machine Learning
summary: Group overview of ML-accelerated discovery and inference, for the Berkeley astrophysics roundtable.
notes: Internal multi-speaker Berkeley event (cf. berkeley-roundtable-2025); date from the deck title slide. Deck in local archive.

title: Astrophysical Machine Learning
summary: Astrophysical machine learning for a College of Letters & Science faculty roundtable.
notes: Internal Berkeley L&S event; date from the deck title slide. Deck in local archive.
